what: Splitting a system so each part owns one responsibility (UI, business logic, data) and knows little about the others.
when: From the start of any non-trivial app, and whenever a file or module starts doing several unrelated jobs.
why: Keeps changes local: you can rework one concern without breaking the rest, and an AI agent can work one part without holding the whole system in context.

what: A monolith is one deployable app; microservices split it into many small independently-deployed services.
when: Start monolith; reach for services only when teams, scaling, or domains genuinely need independent deploys.
why: Microservices trade local simplicity for operational complexity — premature splitting is a top cause of stalled side projects.

what: Components communicate by emitting and reacting to events rather than calling each other directly.
when: When parts must stay decoupled, work asynchronously, or many consumers react to one action.
why: Decouples producers from consumers so you can add behavior without touching the emitter — but makes flow harder to trace.

what: An operation that produces the same result whether it runs once or many times.
when: Any retry-able action: payment capture, webhooks, queue handlers, migrations.
why: Networks retry; without idempotency a retry double-charges or duplicates data. It's the difference between safe and dangerous retries.

what: Storing the result of expensive work so repeat requests are fast.
when: When the same expensive read happens often and the data tolerates being slightly stale.
why: Huge speedups, but cache invalidation is famously hard — a stale cache shows wrong data, so always have a clear invalidation rule.

what: How a UI tracks and shares changing data (local state, a store like Zustand/Redux, server cache).
when: As soon as data is shared across components or must survive navigation.
why: Tangled state is the #1 source of vibecoded UI bugs; a clear store with single sources of truth keeps it predictable.

what: A self-contained, reusable piece of UI with its own markup, state, and styling.
when: Whenever a piece of UI repeats or grows complex enough to name.
why: Components let you compose UIs from small tested parts instead of one giant file — the core of maintainable frontends.

what: A Single-Page App renders in the browser; Server-Side Rendering produces HTML on the server first.
when: SSR for content/SEO/first-paint-sensitive sites; SPA for app-like tools behind a login.
why: Affects SEO, perceived speed, and complexity — choosing wrong means rework or a slow first load.

what: Attaching client-side interactivity to server-rendered HTML after it loads.
when: In SSR/SSG apps that need interactivity.
why: A hydration mismatch (server HTML ≠ client render) causes flicker and bugs — keep server and client output identical.

what: Named design values (colors, spacing, radii) defined once and referenced everywhere, often as CSS variables.
when: Any app with theming or a consistent visual language.
why: Change a token, restyle the whole app; hardcoded values make theming and consistency nearly impossible.

what: Designing so people using keyboards, screen readers, or with low vision can use your app.
when: Always — bake it in, don't bolt it on.
why: It's the right thing, often legally required, and the same semantics (labels, roles, focus) also make UIs more robust and testable.

what: An HTTP API organized around resources and verbs (GET/POST/PUT/DELETE) returning JSON.
when: Most CRUD-style backends and public APIs.
why: Ubiquitous and cacheable; predictable conventions mean clients and tools just work.

what: A query language where clients ask for exactly the fields they need from one endpoint.
when: When clients need flexible, nested data and you want to avoid many REST round-trips.
why: Reduces over/under-fetching, but adds server complexity and caching is harder than REST.

what: An Object-Relational Mapper lets you query a database with code objects instead of raw SQL.
when: Most apps with a relational database and CRUD-heavy access.
why: Speeds development and prevents some injection bugs, but hides SQL — watch for N+1 queries and learn to drop to raw SQL when needed.

what: Versioned, ordered scripts that evolve a database schema over time.
when: From the first schema change — never edit the live schema by hand.
why: Makes schema changes reproducible and reversible across environments; ad-hoc changes cause drift and data loss.

what: Authentication is who you are (login); authorization is what you're allowed to do (permissions).
when: Any app with users or protected data.
why: Conflating them is a classic security hole — being logged in is not the same as being allowed; check both on every protected action.

what: Capping how many requests a client can make in a window.
when: On any public or expensive endpoint, especially auth and API calls.
why: Protects against abuse, runaway costs, and accidental loops; without it one client can exhaust your service or budget.

what: An HTTP callback another service sends you when an event happens (e.g. a payment succeeds).
when: To react to events from third parties without polling.
why: Real-time and efficient, but you must verify signatures and handle retries idempotently or you'll process events twice.

what: A buffer that holds tasks so producers and workers run at their own pace.
when: For slow, spiky, or retry-able background work (emails, image processing).
why: Decouples request latency from heavy work and smooths spikes; without it slow tasks block requests or get lost on crash.

what: Continuous Integration runs tests on every change; Continuous Delivery/Deployment ships passing changes automatically.
when: As soon as more than a throwaway script — even solo.
why: Catches breakage before it ships and removes risky manual deploys; the safety net that lets you move fast without fear.

what: A persistent two-way connection between browser and server for live updates.
when: Real-time features: chat, presence, live dashboards, collaborative editing.
why: Pushes updates instantly without polling, but you must handle reconnects and scaling across servers.

what: A task scheduled to run automatically at fixed times.
when: Recurring maintenance: backups, digests, cleanup, syncs.
why: Automates routine work, but silent failures are easy — always log and alert on a missed or failed run.

what: A lightweight, isolated package of your app plus its dependencies that runs the same everywhere.
when: To make deploys reproducible across machines and environments.
why: Kills 'works on my machine'; the foundation most modern deploy platforms build on.

what: Running code as functions that the platform scales and bills per request, with no servers to manage.
when: Spiky or low-traffic workloads, glue code, and event handlers.
why: Zero idle cost and auto-scaling, but cold starts, time limits, and vendor lock-in are real trade-offs.

what: A network of edge servers that cache and serve your static assets close to users.
when: Any app with global users or static files (images, JS, CSS).
why: Dramatically faster loads and less origin load; not using one leaves easy performance on the table.

what: Config and secrets passed to the app at runtime instead of hardcoded in source.
when: For anything that differs by environment or must stay secret (API keys, DB URLs).
why: Keeps secrets out of git and lets one build run in dev/staging/prod — hardcoding secrets is a top security failure.

what: Logs, metrics, and traces that let you understand what a running system is doing.
when: Before you need it — set up basic logging/alerting from day one.
why: You can't fix what you can't see; without it production issues are guesswork and outages drag on.

what: Running two production environments and switching traffic from old (blue) to new (green) instantly.
when: When you need zero-downtime deploys and a fast rollback.
why: Switch back instantly if the new version misbehaves — much safer than deploying in place.

what: The smallest version of a product that delivers real value and tests your core assumption.
when: At the start of any new product, before building the full vision.
why: Ships learning fast and avoids months building something nobody wants — the antidote to over-building.

what: The point where a product satisfies a real market need strongly enough that usage pulls itself forward.
when: The goal of early-stage product work; measure before scaling.
why: Scaling before fit burns money on a leaky bucket; finding fit is the real early milestone.

what: The revenue and cost of a single unit (customer or transaction).
when: Before scaling spend, and when pricing.
why: If you lose money per unit, growth makes it worse; positive unit economics is what makes a business viable.

what: The rate at which customers stop using or paying for your product.
when: Track from your first paying users.
why: High churn quietly cancels out growth; reducing it is usually cheaper than acquiring new users.

what: How you charge: one-time, subscription, usage-based, freemium, tiered.
when: Early, and revisit as you learn what customers value.
why: Pricing shapes who buys, your revenue, and your incentives — it's a product decision, not an afterthought.

what: The plan for how you reach, convince, and onboard your first customers.
when: Alongside building — distribution is as important as the product.
why: A great product nobody hears about fails; how you'll reach users should shape what you build.

what: A shared library of components, patterns, and rules that keep a product visually and behaviorally consistent.
when: Once a product has more than a handful of screens or contributors.
why: Consistency builds trust and speeds work; without it every screen drifts and re-solves the same problems.

what: A low-fidelity sketch of a screen's layout and content, without final visuals.
when: Early, to agree on structure before investing in pixels.
why: Cheap to change; settling layout and flow before visuals avoids expensive late rework.

what: How content and features are organized, labeled, and navigated.
when: Whenever an app grows beyond a few screens.
why: Good IA means users find things; bad IA makes a feature-rich product feel confusing and empty.

what: Using size, color, spacing, and contrast to guide the eye to what matters most.
when: On every screen, especially dense ones.
why: Tells users where to look first; flat hierarchy makes everything compete and nothing land.

what: The path a user takes through screens to complete a goal.
when: When designing any multi-step task (signup, checkout, setup).
why: Mapping the flow exposes dead ends and friction before you build them.

what: The first-run experience that gets a new user to their first success.
when: For any product where the value isn't obvious in five seconds.
why: Most users decide to stay or leave in the first session; weak onboarding wastes everything upstream of it.

what: What a screen shows before there's any data (no projects, no results yet).
when: Every list, table, or pane that can start empty.
why: A blank screen feels broken; a good empty state explains what goes here and how to start.

what: Showing only what's needed now and revealing advanced options on demand.
when: When a feature has many options but most users need a few.
why: Keeps the common path simple without removing power — reduces overwhelm.

what: Helping users understand and fix a problem instead of dead-ending on an error.
when: Every failure path: bad input, offline, server error.
why: Errors are inevitable; a clear message plus a way forward turns frustration into a recoverable moment.

what: Immediate, clear response to a user action (loading, success, error states).
when: Every action that isn't instantaneous.
why: Without feedback users repeat actions or assume it's broken; the classic 'button that silently does nothing' bug.
